title: Recognizing the contributions of Clela Rorex, pioneering county clerk who advanced civil rights for all couples seeking to be married in 1975.
plain_summary: This bill honors Clela Rorex, a county clerk from Colorado who did something very important for civil rights in 1975. She was the first person in the United States to give a marriage license to a same-sex couple, which was 40 years before it became legal everywhere in the country. Even though some people threatened her for doing this, she kept fighting for the rights of LGBTQ people her whole life. She died in 2022, and now Congress wants to recognize her brave work for equal rights.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes the contributions of Clela Rorex, a county clerk who helped advance civil rights for all couples seeking to be married in nineteen seventy-five.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: Clela Ann Rorex was born in Denver on July twenty-third, nineteen forty-three. Her mother Ruby was a dance and theater teacher. Her father Cecil served as Clerk of Routt County, Colorado, for thirty years. Clela grew up in Steamboat Springs. She earned her bachelor's degree from the University of Colorado Boulder in nineteen seventy-three and a master's degree in Public Administration from the University of Colorado Denver in nineteen eighty-one. At age thirty-one, Clela became Boulder County Clerk and Recorder in January nineteen seventy-five. In nineteen seventy-five, when a same-sex couple requested a marriage license in Boulder County, Clela consulted the assistant district attorney and learned that Colorado State laws did not specifically prohibit granting a marriage license to a same-sex couple. As a newly elected county clerk, Clela issued a marriage license to Dave McCord and Dave Zamora, the first marriage license issued to a same-sex couple in the United States.

sections.title: Clela's Actions and Consequences
sections.text: In two thousand sixteen, Clela was quoted as saying that after being deeply involved in the women's rights movements, she could not deny a right to anyone else because it was not her job to legislate morality. Over the next month, Clela issued five more marriage licenses to same-sex couples. National news outlets covered Clela's groundbreaking story, after which she reported receiving a flood of death threats and condemnation in hundreds of letters and phone calls to the Boulder County Clerk's office. Clela issued marriage licenses to six same-sex couples in Boulder County, Colorado, in April nineteen seventy-five, forty years before the Supreme Court legalized same-sex marriage nationwide in two thousand fifteen. In two thousand fourteen, a series of court rulings cleared the way for same-sex marriages in Colorado.

sections.title: Continued Advocacy and Recognition
sections.text: Despite the threats and for decades, Clela continued her advocacy efforts on behalf of the LGBTQ community (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and queer people) including marching, volunteering, and donating to LGBTQ efforts. Clela was seventy-eight years old when she died on June nineteenth, two thousand twenty-two, in Longmont, Colorado. Due to her advocacy for human rights, Boulder County, Colorado, declared July twenty-third, two thousand twenty-two, as Clela Rorex Day. Clela should be recognized for her leadership as a national civil rights leader, paving the way for countless individuals.

sections.title: House Resolution
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives recognizes the contributions of Clela Rorex as a pioneer for civil rights and same-sex marriage. The House recognizes the respect and bravery Clela had when issuing the first same-sex marriage license in the United States. The House recognizes the courage Clela exhibited following the threats she received when she issued marriage licenses to same-sex couples. The House expresses support for the designation of Clela Rorex Day.
